content.text: Cracks in air-dry clay almost always come from
differential drying

. One part of the piece loses moisture faster than another, the faster part shrinks, and the slower part cannot keep up. The stress opens a crack. The fix is not to slow drying down in general, but to make drying even: consistent wall thickness, no exposed thin edges, no direct airflow, and controlled timing for joins.

materials.name: Cotton cloth or thin tea towel for covering

content.text: Preparation

The best time to prevent cracks is at the building stage, not during drying. Before you start any project, commit to two rules: keep wall thickness consistent throughout, and plan your drying surface. Have a flat, non-porous board, a cotton cloth, and a clear workspace ready before you touch the clay.

Build walls and slabs to an even thickness throughout. Use guide sticks (two same-thickness strips of wood on either side of the clay) when rolling slabs to guarantee consistent depth. Check pinch pots and coil builds by pressing lightly around the outside: thick spots feel hard and unyielding; thin spots flex. Even them out before proceeding.

Keep rim and edge thickness the same as wall thickness. Rims that have been thinned during shaping are a common crack point. If the rim has become thinner than the wall, add a thin coil of clay and blend it in while the piece is still fresh.
When building with
score and slip

joins, leave the joined piece to rest for 20-30 minutes before moving on. During this time the two joined sections stiffen together and reach a similar moisture level. Moving the piece too soon pulls apart joins that have not had time to bond fully.

Place the finished piece on a flat board and cover loosely with a cotton cloth for the first 12 hours. The cloth traps a small amount of humidity around the piece, slowing surface drying and allowing the inside and outside to lose moisture at a similar rate. Do not use plastic at this stage: it seals too completely and can cause the surface to become sticky rather than firm.
Keep the piece away from direct airflow: open windows, fans, and radiators will dry one side faster than the other. Place it in a still corner of the room.

For pieces with spans, arches, or overhanging sections, support them physically during drying. Crumpled newspaper, foam offcuts, or dry scraps of clay placed underneath prevent the unsupported clay from sagging and stretching as it stiffens. Remove the supports once the piece reaches the
leather-hard
stage and can hold its own weight.

After 12-24 hours, remove the cloth and flip the piece upside down onto a fresh dry board if possible. This equalises moisture loss from both sides and prevents the base from staying wetter than the top for longer than necessary.
What success looks like

The fully dry piece is one uniform pale colour from surface to edge with no darker damp patches. No cracks are visible on joins, rims, or spanning sections. The piece sits flat without rocking on a flat surface.
Common mistakes
Drying near a radiator or sunny windowsill to speed things up: this causes surface cracking within hours. Room temperature drying is always better.
Leaving a piece on a non-porous surface (glass, plastic board) for the entire drying period: the base stays trapped and wet while the top dries, causing the base to crack.
Uneven wall thickness: thin sections crack first, and the crack can run into thicker areas nearby.

items.fix: If the crack is fresh and the clay still has moisture, press the edges back together firmly and smooth with a damp finger. If fully dry, fill with clay paste (slip) and let it dry slowly before sanding.
items.cause: The two joined sections dried at different rates or the join did not bond fully before moving
items.problem: Crack appears at a join line

items.fix: For next time, cover for a full 24 hours. Surface cracks already present in a dry piece can be filled with clay slip, sanded back, and sealed once fully dry.
items.cause: Surface dried much faster than the interior due to airflow or heat
items.problem: Spider-web surface cracks across the whole piece

items.fix: Transfer to a slightly porous surface such as a cloth-covered board and flip the piece after 12 hours to allow even moisture loss from both sides.
items.cause: Base trapped on a non-porous surface for too long
items.problem: Piece warps and cracks at the base

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
